era_context: Early 16th century: the High Renaissance in Italy; the Portuguese maritime empire expanding in the Indian Ocean under Manuel I; the Ottoman Empire consolidating power under Selim I's reign (beginning 1512); the Ming Dynasty in China under the Zhengde Emperor; the Safavid Empire in Persia under Ismail I; the Spanish Reconquista recently completed (1492), with Spain consolidating colonial holdings in the Americas and North Africa.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Sub-Saharan Africa (limited written records; Portuguese coastal contacts not yet systematically documented)
Interior of the Americas (Aztec and Inca records exist but are fragmentary; Spanish conquest narratives dominate European sources)

events.title: Death of Pope Alexander VI; Election of Pope Julius II
events.description: Pope Alexander VI died on 18 August 1503 (not 1506; see disconfirming evidence). However, Pope Julius II (Giuliano della Rovere) was elected on 31 October 1503 and was actively consolidating papal power in 1506. In 1506, Julius II laid the foundation stone of the new St. Peter's Basilica on 18 April, marking a watershed moment in Renaissance patronage and the beginning of a massive architectural and artistic project that would define the century. This event symbolized the papacy's temporal and spiritual ambitions and initiated the financial pressures that would eventually trigger the Protestant Reformation.

sources.name: Vasari, Lives of the Artists; contemporary papal records
Archaeological evidence from St. Peter's Basilica excavations

events.certainty_note: The foundation stone laying is documented in multiple contemporary sources and confirmed by archaeological evidence.

events.title: Portuguese expedition under Tristão da Cunha reaches the Indian Ocean; establishment of Portuguese control in East Africa
events.description: Tristão da Cunha led a Portuguese fleet that captured Sofala (in present-day Mozambique) and Kilwa in 1505–1506, consolidating Portuguese control over East African coastal trade routes. These conquests were part of Vasco da Gama's broader strategy (initiated after his 1498 voyage) to monopolize the Indian Ocean spice trade by controlling key ports. The capture of Kilwa in particular was brutal and marked a turning point in European military dominance in the region, displacing Arab and Swahili merchant networks that had dominated for centuries.
key_figures: Vasco da Gama (Portuguese viceroy in India, 1505–1509)

sources.name: Gaspar Correia, Lendas da Índia (compiled early 16th century, based on contemporary accounts)
Portuguese royal archives and contemporary dispatches

events.certainty_note: Multiple Portuguese sources confirm the dates and outcomes; archaeological evidence from Kilwa and Sofala corroborates occupation and fortification.

events.title: Michelangelo completes the marble statue of David
events.description: Michelangelo Buonarroti completed his monumental marble sculpture of David in early 1504 (not 1506; see disconfirming evidence). However, the statue was unveiled and installed in the Piazza della Signoria on 8 September 1504, and remained a focal point of Florentine civic identity throughout 1506 and beyond. The work represents the apex of Renaissance humanism and technical mastery, depicting the biblical hero as an idealized male nude in classical contrapposto. Its public prominence in 1506 made it one of the most influential artworks of the era.
key_figures: The Opera del Duomo (Florence Cathedral workshop)

sources.name: Michelangelo's letters and contemporary Florentine records

events.certainty_note: Completion date is 1504; the statue was publicly displayed and celebrated throughout 1506. Included here for its cultural significance during this year.

events.title: Columbus's fourth and final voyage ends; return to Spain
events.description: Christopher Columbus returned to Spain in November 1504 after his fourth voyage to the Americas (1502–1504), which had explored the coasts of present-day Honduras, Nicaragua, Costa Rica, and Panama. The voyage was marked by severe storms, shipworm damage, and conflict with indigenous peoples. Columbus was in poor health and had lost royal favor; he spent his remaining years petitioning the Spanish crown for recognition and financial compensation. This voyage marked the effective end of Columbus's exploration career and the beginning of systematic Spanish colonization under other leaders.
key_figures: Queen Isabella I of Castile (deceased 26 November 1504)

sources.name: Columbus's letters and journals; Ferdinand Columbus's biography of his father

events.certainty_note: Return date is well-documented in multiple sources. Columbus died in 1506 (see disconfirming evidence).

events.title: Death of Christopher Columbus
events.description: Christopher Columbus died on 20 May 1506 in Valladolid, Spain, at approximately 54 years of age. He died in relative obscurity and financial hardship, having failed to secure the titles and revenues promised by the Spanish crown. His death marked the end of an era of personal exploration and the beginning of systematic colonial expansion. Columbus's legacy was immediately contested: some celebrated him as a visionary discoverer, while others (particularly indigenous peoples) recognized him as the initiator of conquest and catastrophe. His remains were moved multiple times over subsequent centuries, and disputes over his burial site persisted into the modern era.

sources.name: Ferdinand Columbus, Life of the Admiral (biography of his father, compiled early 16th century)
Spanish royal records and contemporary chronicles

events.certainty_note: Death date is well-documented, though the exact location (Valladolid vs. Seville) has been debated by historians. Most modern scholarship accepts Valladolid.

events.title: Zhengde Emperor of Ming China begins personal rule; dismissal of regent Liu Jin
events.description: The Zhengde Emperor (r. 1505–1521), who ascended the throne as a child, began to assert personal authority in 1506 after the death of his grandmother, the Empress Dowager. However, the eunuch Liu Jin, who had served as regent and wielded enormous power, was dismissed and executed in 1510 (not 1506). In 1506, the court was in transition, with the young emperor beginning to show interest in personal governance and military affairs. This period marked the beginning of the Zhengde Emperor's reign as an active (if often erratic) ruler, in contrast to the regency period.

sources.name: Ming Shilu (Official Records of the Ming Dynasty)

events.certainty_note: The transition of power in 1506 is documented in Ming records, though the exact date of Liu Jin's fall is 1510. The year 1506 marks the beginning of the emperor's personal rule.

disconfirming_evidence: 1. Pope Alexander VI died in 1503, not 1506. Julius II was elected in 1503 and was already pope in 1506. The foundation stone of St. Peter's was laid in 1506, but this is often conflated with Julius II's election. 2. Michelangelo's David was completed and unveiled in 1504, not 1506. While it remained culturally significant in 1506, the creation date is earlier. 3. Columbus died in 1506, but the exact date (20 May) and location (Valladolid vs. Seville) have been debated by historians; some sources place his death in Seville. 4. The Zhengde Emperor's personal rule began in 1505–1506, but the major political upheaval (Liu Jin's execution) occurred in 1510, not 1506. The year 1506 represents a transition, not a decisive break.
historiographic_note: The year 1506 is well-documented in European sources (Italian Renaissance, Portuguese maritime expansion, Spanish colonial affairs) but less thoroughly recorded in non-European regions. Chinese sources (Ming Shilu) provide detailed records of court politics. Ottoman and Safavid sources are sparse for this specific year. Sub-Saharan African and interior American sources are minimal; Portuguese and Spanish colonial narratives dominate. The historical record is heavily weighted toward elite political and cultural events in Europe and the Mediterranean; economic and social history of non-elite populations is underrepresented.

graph_edges.note: St. Peter's construction and papal financial pressures contributed to the sale of indulgences, which triggered Luther's 95 Theses and the Protestant Reformation.
Portuguese East African conquests were part of the broader strategy initiated by Vasco da Gama's 1498 voyage to monopolize Indian Ocean trade.
Columbus's death marked the end of his personal exploration era and the beginning of systematic Spanish colonization of the Americas.
The Zhengde Emperor's assertion of personal rule in 1506 led to the dismissal and execution of the eunuch regent Liu Jin in 1510.
